text:
We stumbled onto Maynard's while were in town moving my daughter into University.  I am so glad we did.  We ended up eating three meals there during our short Tucson stay.

The first night we poked our head in, were greeted kindly by the hostess and really liked the vibe and the menu .  We checked over at Hotel Congress which looked really fun, too, but Maynard's Kitchen won us over so that's where we ended up.  We sat at the bar where we were met Eddie, our new best friend!  He gave great suggestions, showed wonderful bar knowledge and proceeded to completely erase our 6 hour drive with some beautiful hand-crafted cocktail.  I'm not going to remember all the names, but I had one with watermelon and my boyfriend had a tequila one called the Heritage.  Mine was good but his was transformative.  The cilantro made it pop!  How Eddie managed simple and complex at the same time, I still don't know!  We then grazed our way through several starters.  The mussels were really good and fresh but it was the tomato and grilled watermelon salad that made us sit up and take notice!  Wow!!!  And then, the steak tartare!  It was one of the best things I've ever had.  Oh, time for more cocktails.  The time I had the East End and I was on Cloud 9!  It was just an incredible blend of gin, honeydew (fresh) and lemon grass.  So good!  We had already ordered the Poutine and really didn't need it.  As far as poutine goes, it was really, really good, topped with grilled foie gras.  But I had already heard what the dessert was so we just had a few nibbles them moved on to the Meyer Lemon Goat Cheese Cheesecake.  It was a work of art!

The next morning, daughter wasn't up early, so we went off on breakfast on our own.  I had remember seeing beignets on the menu so we headed back to Maynard's Kitchen.  The hostess remembered us and greeted us like old friends.  Eddie wasn't there but I ordered a virgin bloody mary (too busy of a day for the real deal) and again, it was incredible.  I am pretty sure it had smoked paprika in it.  It also had mint, cilantro, a gherkin, an olive a cucumber slice and an olive stuffed with Blue Cheese.  Sounds like a lot but it hit the spot!  We ordered the Beignets, that were light as cooton puffs,  the red velvet pancakes a la carte that came with smoked maple syrup (wow!!!) and the baked eggs.  I am not sure how they were made but we both agreed it was one of the best things we've ever tasted for breakfast.  Gruyere, white wine, morel mushrooms and spinach (I think), with two poached eggs nestled all in a small cast iron skillet.  Our server was Dave and he couldn't have been nicer or taken better care of us.  He also let us know that not only was Eddie nice (our bartender form the night before) he was also an award winning mixologist.  I can see why!

Our last day, heading out of town, we stopped at Maynard's Market to pick up something to take home to my son, and seeing the already made sandwiches in the gourmet case, we decided to just have lunch there.  I had brie and turkey on ciabatta and he had some sort of amazing club with bacon, pickles and who knows what else.  It looked great.  We grabbed a great organic hard cider to split and some chips and salsa and we were in heaven. I am sorry I don't know the name of the girl who waited on us but again, so nice and friendly.  I know the Market is going to change somewhat in the near future but I hope not too much. 

We will definitely be back sooner rather than later!

(a side note - while there for brunch, a family came in with kids.  There are crayons and butcher paper on the table and plenty of good things to choose from but they left.   The server was more than gracious about all of it.  They looked more like the ihop type anyway and it was definitely their loss.  I wouldn't have hesitated to bring my kids there!)

text: Went there with my husband and a friend. We waited 15 minutes for our drinks. After we finally got them we ordered our food. It took them over an hour to serve us. The waitress never bothered to check in on us the whole time we where waiting. We got our food and my chicken salad had a long black hair in it. I had to wait 10 more minutes to flag down our waitress . Told her about the hair and showed her , her responds was ok. She took the salad and returned with a new one . Not once did she say sorry or let me tell her that I did want any salad at all. Ended up paying for a salad that I never ate. We will not be returning.

text: There's a group of us that go Tuesday evenings after "fighter practice" out at Reid Park.  In short, the staff is friendly and charming! Sometimes a bit slow, but they make up for it in the quality of their service, drinks, and food.  Speaking of, like I said, service is pretty darned good, the servers know their customers and their quirks and special wants.  They've made Painkiller (rum drink I discovered in the Caribbean Islands earlier in the year), for me that is pretty darned closed to the original and one of my favorite drinks, and they have pretty fabulous (and HUGE) salads, as well as a variety of tasty burgers, and messy-but-oh-so-yummy wings!

OK here is the gig... been here about a dozen times and eaten about half those stops.  mostly I there for the adult beverages before UA football and basketball games. Will this time we went in today before the UofA vs Wazzu and I went with my buddy of mine and he told me about the Blue Plate special. 

Well when I hear Blue Plate special I'm thinking yes it's a blue hair dish what the heck is going on here right....?  Well I was unbelievably surprised that it was a creamy New England style clam chowder with chunks of clams in it that were absolutely huge and delicious.  So then I opted for adding the half of the BLT sandwich which we all know BLT is bacon lettuce tomato but they add avocado and cream cheese and holy s*** was it ridiculously awesome but it probably had to do with the 10 slices of bacon that were on it. 

How many times have you ever been to a place an order to BLT and there's two slices of witty/soggy horrible bacon on it ???

Well this wasn't the case it, was good solid slabs of bacon not real fatty and good portion of meat cooked perfectly....and it was absolutely killer...

I can't can't wait to go back for more belly of the pig and chunks of clams!

text:
Maynards! I LOVE YOUR FOOD. I have eaten indoors and outdoors, and the service has always been divine. My most recent experience was about a month ago. My husband and I were having our last date night before I left for a trip and he left for work. 

We came in on a Monday night. It was not crowded, but it was pretty late for dinner. I think we were there around 8PM. We ordered the Poutine which are fries made in duck fat, with duck confit, foie gras, cheese curds, and topped with duck gravy. That plate in itself is absolutely breathtakingly fabulously delicious. I am not going overboard with the description. Just order the Poutine... it is mind-blowing.

The waiter recommended a wine. I can't remember, but it was delicious. Maynard's has a very good drink selection as far as cocktails and wine go. I ordered the pork shank. It was pretty ridiculous in size. There were so many flavors in that dish that it was hard to combine them all in one bite. It was definitely a winner in my book. The portion size was huge, so I was definitely unable to even make it look like I tried to eat it. Nevertheless, my experience was wonderful.

If you are looking for an upscale atmosphere and wonderful food, check out Maynards. You won't be disappointed!

text:
If I was only rating the food I might give this place five stars. We has the tikki masala, the lamb samosas, half a tandoori chicken, and some garlic naan and everything was fantastic. If you like flavorful Indian-Paki food, this place is a hit.

The wait staff wasn't all that great however. The young girl that was serving us was very disinterested and disengaged. Don't go in expecting classy service or anything. They have some liquor but don't expect them to know how to prepare your mixed drink of choice unless its as simple as adding ice.  They also have a small wine list; nothing to write home about. I also wouldn't really take advice on any of these wines from the wait staff as I'm not sure they'd be able to help.

To sun up, remember that the food is great. Just go in with the mindset that your service will be poor.

Recently tried this place on a Wednesday night for Geeks who Drink! Service was great - our server Melissa never let our drinks get too empty and always accommodating. I ordered the trident burger, MR with a side of sweet potato fries. The burger was delicious and cooked to order! The fries were a perfect texture, crisp and not soggy. 

The venue itself is a little small for the amount of people that were there on a busy night as it got really stuffy and hot. They only have 2 stalls in their womens restrooms so there was almost always a line. Everything was very clean, however, despite the vast number of bodies in the room.

text:
I had gone to Native Seeds next door for seeds for my garden and wondered about Amelia Grey's next door. Then when I drove past a couple weeks ago and noticed it was now occupied by a new establishment called Prep & Pastry I checked out the menu online. Everything looked amazing!

I finally got down there on Monday this week and I asked the waiter/possible owner(?) to pick something for me. He picked the brioche French toast stuffed with a berry compote and freshly whipped cream. It was decadent, sweet but balanced, and served with real maple syrup. I also ordered a cup of chamomile tea and they brought it out in a personal size French press. So cute! I also got another to go, at no added cost! 

I also loved the interior. It has a farmhouse or old barn feel about it with chalkboard painted walls covered in cute drawings and quotes. The decor makes you feel good, with so much natural light coming in through the many windows in the building. 

I will definitely be back and next time I'm trying something savory!

text:
Went to Barrio  Brewing on St. Patty's Day eve. Traveling into the heart of Tucson you get a feel for the city coming from the Oracle Valley. This place is in an industrial part of town. If your an out of towner, your a little apprehensive about where your going to. From the outside it looks like a rundown warehouse with a scrap metal look. It took a little bit for me to warm up to the look but after my experience I became a fan of Barrio.
My wife had a veggie burger that was average but the toppings of guacamole, cheese and horseradish sauce made her burger tasty.
I had the California burger with mushrooms added. Nice size burger, very well cooked. The fries for me were a little below average. They need to leave the skins on the fries to have some body to them.
We had there stout and porter. Both beers were pretty good with the stout being a little more creamy.
They have a shuffleboard table that took me back. Haven't seen one in years. We played and had a youngster watching us play and kept him entertained. It was fun.
We recommend Barrio to locals and out of towners.

text: We went for Sunday breakfast. We ordered the French Toast, Pork Belly Sandwich and Biscuits and Gravy and a dossant. My wife ordered loose leaf tea and I had coffee. I'll start with the service: top notch, really just amazing. You'll notice Phil from Jax Kitchen as a familiar face and he does an excellent job as does all of the other wait staff. Very, very attentive. The food is excellent overall. The biscuits were light and flaky (clearly handmade) and the gravy was amazing. Creamy, flavorful and just right. My pork belly sandwich was equally good. I could have done without the shredded apple and the pork belly didn't seem braised but it was still very good. The french toast was the best! Buttery, flavorful and well executed. We tried to have the original "Cronut" ala D. Ansel in New York, but it didn't work out. I think this would be a worthy substitute.

attributes.BusinessParking: {u'valet': False, u'garage': False, u'street': None, u'lot': None, u'validated': None}

text:
Probably the most confusing service I've ever encountered in Tucson. It was our first time here and just moved in down the street so, why not. We initially got our drinks right away (being pregnant I only had water). But then things started flying out of the kitchen and we hear some man screaming in there who then comes out and slams stuff down on the bar.. like having a real meltdown in the middle of his shift. Crazy. 

I asked for a refill on my water and the bartender grabbed a pitcher, slammed it down in front of me and then said "you can have it then." WHAT!!!  Ok ignored that too, we went outside to the batting cages which were really neat and busy but not overly crowded. 

We got hungry so I grabbed a menu and placed my order at the bar- sort of. I started with my guys food order and she just turned around and said "ok" totally cutting me off as she walked away.. I had to yell over the volume of the place wait I'm not done!! Then when the food came a confused looking young girl who ran our food looked at me like I was insane for asking for ketchup and mustard for a hot dog. Which when she brought the mustard it was clogged so bad I couldn't even get it out, gross. 

Overall, food was good and at least I had my pitcher of water, lol. I grabbed rounds of beers for the boys a couple times  to get out of the smoking area temporarily and the service was quick and to the point. 

When it came time to close our tab the bartender was SO sweet and nice, obviously. I tipped 20% like a normal person and left with no intention of coming back on my own decision.

name: Penca
categories: Mexican, Restaurants, Nightlife, Bars, Venues & Event Spaces, Event Planning & Services
attributes.Ambience: {'touristy': False, 'hipster': False, 'romantic': False, 'divey': False, 'intimate': False, 'trendy': True, 'upscale': False, 'classy': True, 'casual': False}
attributes.GoodForMeal: {'dessert': False, 'latenight': False, 'lunch': None, 'dinner': True, 'brunch': None, 'breakfast': False}
text:
My wife and I have had some wonderful meals at Penca, but it's become so painfully inconsistent that I feel obligated to write this critical review.

Our waiter tonight was named Alex. He was inattentive and slow - it took 15 minutes for our drinks to arrive after ordering them (one of which contained a gnat), and this was on a quiet weekday night with only 3 or 4 other tables seated. He also did not put our appetizer order in, though it was ordered clearly and he seemed to acknowledge it. This was disappointing because the appetizer--the Queso Fundido, a hearty melted cheese and sausage dish--was one of our favorites!

The food we did receive was subpar compared to other visits. I ordered the Mole de Guajolote, described as "braised turkey in mole poblano, pickled gray squash, rice, tortilla." What I got was a bowl of rice topped with lightly-sauced shredded turkey and 3 tortillas on the side. This lacked any sort of wow factor, whether in flavor, ingredients, or presentation, and seemed completely overpriced at $21. 

My wife ordered the Costillas de Res, "braised beef short ribs, ancho chile glaze, mexican squash, toasted whole red hominy folded with fresh goat cheese." The ribs were tender but underseasoned, while the hominy, usually a stellar side at Penca, was undercooked and oversalted instead of the tender, creamy bites of heaven it can be.

This experience was not helped by the fact that the front door was broken, causing monsoon season winds to blow through the restaurant and scatter menus, while the leaky roof also dripped in several places. Sorry Penca, but we're just not into you any more.

name: St. Philipâ€™s Plaza
categories: Shopping, Restaurants, Shopping Centers, Nightlife
text:
Gotta love Saint Philips! The Farmers Market on Sundays are the best in town. It's a great place to come with the pups and walk around during this and other fun Tucson events the plaza holds. I think I've been to three or four that were a blast. If I remember correctly a couple art events, a Jazz festival, and mini farmers markets.

The plaza is also home to great lunch and dinner spots like Scordato's Pizzeria and Bodega Kitchen and Wine... Not to mention the night life fun at Union for Whiskey Wednesday! I haven't been there myself but I also hear Gadabouts is a great place to get your hair done. 

Lunch, shopping, dinner and a late night scene in the foothills! Plus, a clean and unkept place with a peaceful ambiance... Not too much to complain about! You almost forget you're in the Arizona desert.

text:
I was in Tuscon for work for a couple days. I was craving pasta. I knew Claim Jumper served pasta so I went on in. The waitress was nice, the server, the host.. Everything was cool. That is, until I ate the pasta I ordered. I got the black tie pasta.

Bruh... This pasta was sooo overcooked! It was the bow tie pasta. They weren't bow ties anymore. They were blankets!!! They were so overcooked and soft that the middle of the pasta had detached. It was just a bunch of blanket pastas. 

Waitress walked on by and asked, "how's your food".. I said "Its good" because she was nice and I didn't wanna be a headache. (I should've said something lol. I'm paying for this)

Anyways, I finished my food because I was starving like hell. Won't be going back tho

text:
I wish I could give it more stars, but it just doesn't measure up. The food is bar food, not bad, but not great. The service depends on the visit, I have been a few times, sometimes its great sometimes it just plain sucks. 

The food looks so much better when reading the menu, my mouth waters, when it shows up, there is no pride, or time to make it look nice, and the taste that was described is lack luster.

The location is odd, not in the best part of town, Tucson doesn't have many best areas, but its in a odd location for me. I don't feel safe after dark walking to my car.

The drinks on the other hand are great, which is why I gave it 3 stars. From beer to spirits I would go here for that. The bartenders are great, and knowledgable.

categories: Mexican, Restaurants, Nightlife, Bars, Venues & Event Spaces, Event Planning & Services

text:
Been wanting to try this place, so we decided to go for lunch. 

We both ordered the farmers salad with carne asada which was VERY well put together. The meat used was some of the best and the walnuts were my favorite. I really enjoyed it and would recommend the small because it had me full! 

The chips and salsa we very tasty and well mixed. I am very picky when it comes to salsa (yes, I know it's a very simple mixture) but, a lot of places just can't get it right. It was very tasty but has no kick to it but the flavor was still very satisfying. 

The water was served at room temperature which was kinda gross to me.

The only reason I can't give the place all stars is because we were waiting a whole HOUR for a SALAD. Two of their SMALL farmers salad's took 1 WHOLE HOUR. The tea was only refilled once and sat on empty for most of the time. Our waiter only came once to take our order and that was it. We had to ask a busser for our waiter because it was taking so long. In the end we both enjoyed the salads and were given a discount which was greatly appreciated. I wanted so much to LOVE this place because of how beautiful the decor and how much I liked my salad but I just am still in shock it took an hour for a salad. 

maybe next time Penca, hopefully next time...

They were busy when we arrived in the late afternoon, and a continuous flow of patrons went through their doors. So, maybe we just got the batches that were over salted?

The Chocolate Motherlode Cake was great.

text:
Ordered some take out with a large group and let me first say they do take out perfectly right. They have each person's order separated by check and in different bags so they are easy to find and you know what's in the bag without opening all the containers!

I ordered lamb korma with a veggie pakora and a plain naan.  We fought a little to get some rice (my co-eater had to pull out some language skills on the phone when ordering to get it for us complimentary).

It was pretty good lamb korma.  Very tender, great sauce that I just wanted to keep eating.  A little oily but that was the only downside.  The veggie pakora was good as well and still crispy despite the drive. I thought it was a bit heavy though with too much potato.  The naan was too dry or too thin for my taste.  Baked to the point that I couldn't use it to soak up anything and had to rely solely on the rice to get that yummy sauce onto my taste buds.

I would definitely eat here again though and I am looking forward to my leftovers (always a good sign)!

text: I really should have read reviews before we stopped here. This place has gone down hill in food quality. We ordered zucchini as an appetizer and it was terrible. How lazy is the kitchen staff when they don't even bother to put bread coating on to stay on the zucchini?! For entree Hubbs ordered potato cheese soup and I ordered hickory burger without the bun with shoe string fries. I guess the soup was OK since he didn't complain. Hickory burger was very small but the flavor was fine. All the condiments were sad though. Wilted and brown lettuce, frozen, mushy tomatoes, off tasting pickle chips the bacon, cheese and BBQ sauce were the high point of the burger. The fries are described as salt and pepper shoe strings... Well they are about 50% correct. Hardly any salt or pepper but they were shoestring. I've had a better burger/fry combo at Freddy's steak burger. Our final bill came to 33.48!!!! For what?! These people have some nerve charging this kind of price for subpar food. Don't bother stopping in here for fine dining.

text:
If I could describe this in two words, it would be amazing and affordable! The food here is absolutely delicious! I especially enjoy the Tikka Masala dishes, but the Vindaloo and Rogan Josh are also delightful! I usually enjoy my curry with a good deal of heat, but they will ask what spice level you prefer, so you can get anything from mild-extra spicy!
Along with your curry, you can enjoy rice and/or naan bread. The naan bread is solid, but the rice is incredible! It's super soft and really compliments the curry entrées!
Now, if you find the curry a tad spicy for your tastes, you can cool down with one of the most amazing drinks found in Tucson. Ladies and gentlemen, I am speaking of the legendary Mango Lassi. Now you can get one of these at any Indian restaurant, but there is no Mango Lassi out there like this one. It's fresh, sweet, and creamy and will quench any fire in your mouth from the curry.
TL;DR- order a Tilka Masala dish along with a Mango Lassi for an amazing and affordable meal!

Really decent food for a really decent price.  I love that they have the sandwiches mostly prepared, but undressed.  That makes them nice and fresh even when  they are mostly made ahead of time.

The food bar (salad plus tons of items) looked quite good.  I almost got it, but the sandwich was too good looking to pass up.  Avocado can't be wrong!

I thought charging $1 extra for eggs at lunch is kinda BS since there were tons of people ordering them.  I mean extra charges are for abnormal orders or things that they put away, but the eggs were none of that.

text: This was our 3rd time here. The service has been friendly so we returned. I ordered a sangria. They make these with a cup of shaved ice and out it in a clear glass. The ice formed a frozen film on the glass and when I went to pick up the glass to drink the sangria the glass slipped right out of my hand. My $10 sangria was half in the glass and half in the bar. The bartender admitted the ice forms a coat on the glass (after it was all over the bar) and becomes slippery. He could have told me this when he gave me the drink. text: I have been several times and recommended to visitors.  Everybody has been very pleased. I very rarely eat fried food but was totally happy with the pakoras (spinach and vegetable). We had several curries, mostly lamb and chicken as well as naan and papadam.  A nice assortment of well priced Indian and other beers to match.  Friendly, knowledgeable and efficient service. attributes.Ambience: {'touristy': False, 'hipster': False, 'romantic': False, 'divey': False, 'intimate': False, 'trendy': False, 'upscale': False, 'classy': True, 'casual': False}

text:
We had been anxiously awaiting the opening of this restaurant since we can walk to it from where we live, and have been searching for a really great restaurant on the east side. Sadly, our first experience here was far less then stellar. 

I have to agree with the other reviewer about the ambience. There is none. We are hoping that they opened without having finished decorating the place and that it may get better with a little more time. It is so stark and way too bright in there. But great food can always nullify bad ambience. Alas....

The food was incredibly disappointing. After being told by our waiter that the Jackson burger was the best burger he'd ever eaten, my husband ordered the Jackson burger with fries and I ordered the truffle burger with fries. The truffle burger was $18 and I had to pay an extra $1.50 for a single slice of Swiss cheese. I ordered my burger medium, and it came out almost well done. The "confit potatoes" (fries) were devoid of flavor. While my husbands jackson burger was at least cooked properly, it was also quite flavorless and also had to pay an extra $1.50 for a single slice of cheese on the burger. He was dying to put a little salt on his burger, and shared my opinion on the lack of flavor in the fries. 

After two mediocre-at-best burgers and three beers (combined, not each), we had a $60 check.  Definitely not worth the price we paid. 

We may give it one more chance since it was opening night, but we don't have high hopes for Jacksons.

Was super excited to take my husband here for lunch ... but what a disappointment. The ambiance, decor and service were as good as last time, but the food ... needed some work.

I had seen all the amazing photos of the grilled cheese, so I had to have it. Mine didn't look like the pictures though. The ratio of bread to filler wasn't great and the tomatoes had the hard cores you can't really eat. The soup had a skin on top and was really gelatinous. The sammie and the soup were both luke warm. Husband's meal (the dip) was about the same.

It was a sad, sad day. I'll give it one more go because I was so impressed by my first visit ... fingers crossed this was a fluke!

text:
The name of this pizza joint makes me laugh.  And to the best of my knowledge, they seriously don't have anchovies.  If you dine in, it's a loud, college atmosphere, but it's fun.  The servers are also loud and a little sarcastic, and it's a good spot to go before hitting a few bars.

The cool thing about this place is that they offer out of the norm pizza toppings and the food is really good.  The prices are reasonable too and I've been here quite a few times when I'm in town.  

My favorite pizza's are as follows: the vegetarian, the tabono, the spinach pizza and that mediterranean one with the artichoke and feta- it's delish.

They also have good sandwiches and I really like the toasted ravioli appetizer.

We have two kids attending college in Tucson and have tried breakfast spots all over town. This is our clear favorite. 

First, excellent breakfast cocktails. The mimosa menu alone makes this worth a visit. Second, the food is excellent. My husband loves the Return of the Mac. I prefer the French toast- any of their versions is excellent.  I also love the house made yogurt and granola.

Don't forget to look at the pastry selection!

text: Live this place. My husband and I were looking for a place to have lunch and Maynard's caught our eye. I ordered half. Sloppy Joe with a salad and my husband got the corn-beef. text:
If you like your tacos served with a side of ambiance and an air of pretension then Penca is the place for you. 

Don't get me wrong, the food at Penca is great and the drinks are even better. I usually get the tacos because the homemade corn tortillas are heaven.  As for the cocktails, they are original and very tasty. 

So why 4 stars? Their staff is one of the rudest in Tucson. I've been here 5 or 6 times and only had one server who was pleasant. One night the server suggested I order another cocktail, "I don't think that would be to your taste." 

Another night a friend of mine asked for a wine suggestion. She indicated that she like Sav Blanc, to which the guy reasoned, "well, don't we all?"

On paper the encounters don't sound bad, but it was all about the tone. So if you're willing to put up with some attitude, Penca is great. If not, go somewhere is South Tucson that's twice as good and half the price.

text:
We visited Barrio on the Saturday evening after the Book Festival. The parking lot was packed, which had me a little worried, but the restaurant is huge inside. There were numerous tables available. Unfortunately, we had about a 10-15 minute wait just to get menus and initial service, but things were okay after that slow start.

My husband ordered the beer flight, which he enjoyed immensely. We ordered the beer sponges and the chips and salsa. The beer sponges were AWESOME. Fresh and perfect. The nuclear mustard was especially good--my husband typically hates mustard, but I convinced him to try it and he was surprised and kept eating it after that. The chips were likewise very fresh and delicious, though the salsa was meh.

I ordered a margarita and was disappointed that it tasted like a mix, pure and simple. I wouldn't order it there again.

I had the half Lizzie salad, which turned out to be huge! The overall flavor was great, though it was pretty heavy on onions and bell peppers. It was easy to just eat some and dodge the excess. I was still quite pleased with the salad.

My husband had The Western. He thought it was okay, and the fries were seriously good.

It was Pi Day, and we were sad Barrio did not carry pie. Alas.

Overall, a good experience. We'd go there again whenever we return to Tucson.

text:
We went to Prep & Pastry again this week for a lunch meeting (this was probably our 5th visit), and had more excellent food! Sadly, I can't seem to get away from the French toast, so I'm not one to ask about the sandwiches, but my fiance had the Cubano on this visit and LOVED it, and our dining companions had the dip and a sandwich with egg and brisket on it (I think) and they were very happy with their food. My French toast was not the best I've had there (apple strudel), I wish I had gone with the pineapple upside down cake French toast. Oh, well, this gives me a reason to go back soon - I'm only giving them 4 stars instead of 5 because of less than stellar service on multiple occasions. Not bad, just not good, a lot of running out of liquids and it taking way too long to have them replenished. Still the best place in town to fulfill my need for excellent French toast!!!
*Editing - the cocktails look GOOD, I really wish I were more of a day drinker, because I'd really like to try a couple of them :)

text:
Sigh.  My kingdom for an experience that doesn't degrade over time like a bad drug habit.

I have gotten burned out with my normal venues and in a frantic mental search for where I hadn't been that was relatively close and might be ok, I remembered Amelia Grey's - I keep passing by at times when it's closed or for some reason can't go, and then I forget about it when I'm going...in any case, I'd been meaning to go, and hadn't.  The Native Cafe used to be a favorite and that location has since had trouble keeping an anchor. 

When I arrived on this weekday morning, it was no longer AG's, but Prep & Pastry.  Therefore, part of the imagery which had drawn me - ie, bakery case and cafe feel - was gone.  Instead, it was a nicely appointed restaurant in the ubiquitous chunks of wood and lofty ceiling all the places in Tucson now must do.  Maybe you can't even get a build-out permit in Tucson if you don't use that aesthetic, it's that pervasive.

The first thing I noticed was that the hostess stand is in the wrong place.  It needs to be further to the left so that a table or two can be put in front of the fire place without congesting the entry.  Now that space looks oddly empty and the flow through is wrong.  The second thing I noticed is that this place is heavy on the young men.  A woman may likely greet you and cook, but most of the servers I've seen are men.  That's interesting and definitely creates a vibe which I'm not sure if I like or don't.  

The first day, I was delighted to see that breakfast in some form can be had in the afternoons.  As we know, breakfast is best had at any time of day and should be always on offer, but rarely is.  I had the simple breakfast, which was indeed simple, but for a restaurant of a certain genre, not badly priced at $6.  Except, you know, they scrimp on the toast...artisan toast.  Eye roll.  My biggest complaints were that the eggs were heavy on the salt, the bread was too hard and the bacon too soft.  The coffee was very good, even if my server was too busy talking football and whatnot to refill it timely.  I did feel that I would be getting better service if I was cuter, which is not what you want.  All in all, though, my experience was good and I was sure to come back.

So, I did.  Sadly, it was a downhill experience.  This time I made sure to come in the hours of a full breakfast menu, only to find that it wasn't appreciably more varied.  I ordered the omelette after the waiter had fawned on its inclusion of steak, 5-yr cheddar, mushrooms, tomatoes.  Yum.  It arrived, again oversalted and without a trace of steak.  My toast also arrived hard and completely dry, with no butter or jam.  Sigh.  Coffee was still good.

But, here's what kinda altered my good feelings.  Hey, you know, they're new and I'm well willing to let some bugs get worked out while everyone is figuring out what works. Tweaking is inevitable. So, when I pointed out the lack of a major ingredient in my dish, I wasn't mad.  Just, you know, fix it.  However, the answer I got was that the waiter had made a mistake and read the "beefsteak" tomatoes as steak and tomatoes.  Ok, again, these amusing foibles take place.  And?

And nothing.  Though I would not have ordered the dish as it was and although the waiter was clearly in error, no one seemed to feel that warranted any attention.  Not a comped danish or coffee or jack.  So, what had been a good time by all was now a source of irritation at dumb customer service, which, if I am being frank about my bias, I think may relate to having so many boys in the house.  There's so often just a....lack of social finesse and empathic awareness when boys are in charge (Tony @ MakerHouse is a huge exception to this generality).

It all reminded me of the book, 5 Apology Languages.  I am, most decidedly, in the Accepting Responsibility and Restitution camps.  I am not interested in your flowery debasement, nor is mere acknowledgment enough.  I want to know how you're going to make it right.  Because, otherwise, an apology without making it right just tells me that you're paying lip service and really don't give a shit if I'm happy.  

So, yes, I'll probably go back for limited wants, but it is unlikely to be my go-to.  And guys:  cut it out with the salt, for christ sake.

text:
Great for the classic burger and beer. The atmosphere is pleasant and the train running by every now and again adds a distinct feeling to this brewery. Never experienced poorly done food, and the service is generally quick. Best time to go is during their happy hour, when beers and appetizers are half off.

That being said, their menu descriptions are a bit overzealous (their spiciest wings, described as wicked hot were simply not). They also either don't have an air conditioning unit or it is broken, causing a rather muggy environment in certain seating areas. The worst was on a recent visit where we could smell the sewage coming out of an 'employees only' door after we had finished our meal.

I eat here pretty regularly, though I am fearful for what the summer heat will bring as they don't seem prepared for it.

text:
Small, packed parking lot, you may have to park down the side street (Im sure the neighbors love that). There is usually a wait, but there is a table up front with coffee for you!

Up front here is a bar area for small parties with a giant pastry display to temp you. They also have a lovely looking patio. 

Classic options on the menu for egg-lovers, and those with a sweet tooth. Recently learned you can order a half portion of their french toasts! Thank goodness! The slices are super thick and fluffy and amazing but its best for my waistline if I don't order a huge portion, because I WILL finish it.

text:
Service: 5/5 friendly, prompt. 

Food: 4/5 I had the Biscuit and Gravy with two eggs over easy. The biscuits were a little dry and dense, from a pastry place I was a little (emphasizing on "little") disappointed in this fact.  However the biscuits did have a great flavor. As did the duck gravy which had a good sausage to gravy ratio. The portion of my meal was small but filling and fair for the price. My hot date had the French toast. I must say I was jealous. The bread was fantastic and the banana cream cheese topping was complimenting. 

Ambiance: 5/5. This is not important to me but I know to others it is. The dining room is very open and naturally lit. It is chic and fitting to Tucson's rising scene of fresh local inspired restaurant scene.

name: CataVinos
categories: Wine Tasting Room, Food, Wine Bars, Nightlife, Arts & Entertainment, Bars, Wineries, Beer, Wine & Spirits
text:
I went here for a wine tasting/fundraiser the other day, the atmosphere was interesting, it was a cool little place with a patio and was full of wines that are under $20. I give them 3 stars for selection and the idea/atmosphere of the place. Now onto the wines... I would give them 2 stars for their wine...

As a forewarning, I have recently developed a palate for wine, and have been spoiled with fine German wines from up the street. I am by no means a professional or a snob about wine. I am still in college so I appreciate that all of the wines are under $20, but I feel that you can get a much better bottle of wine for $20-$30 elsewhere. In my opinion, the only reason to buy cheap wine is if you are a wine-o, are having a party, or if you are on a serious budget. 

Moving on, the wine I tasted was crap, most of it was very astringent, green, and harsh. I paid fifteen dollars for the tasting (normally their tastings are $10), expecting some decent wines, but only the last wine was remotely drinkable. Definitely over priced in my opinion. 

Conclusion:

The wine is cheap and I was not impressed with the quality. (If you are having a large tasting, pull your best wines out.) The atmosphere and concept of the place is cool. Go here if you are a poor college student / alcoholic / need bulk wine. If you want a much better bottle of wine for ~$20, go up the street to The Wine Depot on Grant & Alvernon, they have wine tastings for $5 on Friday.

text:
K, I've noticed a trend when in seating for new or remodeled restaurants, and that is the uncomfortable seating.  I have a feeling that the hard seating surfaces are easier to keep clean or something, which is important, but sheesh.  That said, the interior (right down to the hard seating) is freaking awesome.  Look at those high-chairs made out of pipe and wood.  Neat prohibition theme!

Nice selection of beers, and I'd never heard of mules before, but they have 'em and they're tasty!

Had the Wiseguy Fries as an appetizer.  Fries, nacho cheese, BBQ pulled pork (if I remember right), sour creme, and some green onion.  Served in a great big bucket.  Yummy.

I had the bucket burger with the chipotle sauce.  Amazing!  Came with fries... now these are the same fries used in the awesome appetizer, right?  Now I'm a bit of a fry nazi... I want mine more... turgid.  These were... flaccid.  Tasted good, though.  Just not my thing.

We also got a chicken wrap that looked good.  I didn't try it, but it came with chips and salsa.  The salsa is better than the salsa at the Mexican place down the street, and also had a bit of kick.  I expected something more bland from a brewhouse, I guess.  Nice job!

Will totally eat here whenever I want a beer and some yummy stuff to go with  it!

text: Fantastic food! Oh my gosh, those fresh corn tortillas are the best! We sought out Penca as being one of the few places you can find chile en nogada on the menu, but fate landed us there midday and they only serve it for dinner. Balls! Plan B was the tacos and they didn't disappoint. We tried the rajas, cabeza, carnitas, and lengua tacos. We loved them all, and if we ranked them it'd be in the order I just listed. The house michelada was wonderful as well. I'd love to make my way back to this restaurant some day and try more from the menu, and I highly recommend it if you are living in or visiting Tucson!

text: Heard so many awesome things about this place and came with some pretty high expectations. Got there about 15 minutes before lunch started, my bad, so went outside to wait til it started. I didn't understand why they couldn't take our order and wait until 11 to put it in that way we didn't walk back and forth, but not every restaurant thinks of these things. Was very disappointed, stood next to the register to give my order and when someone FINALLY came to take it, he was obviously not trained to take an order. He kept fumbling with the register and had to ask someone where something was. Why wasn't there a proper trainer on hand? Crazy. I ordered two pepperoni pizzas and a grilled cheese, there were 3 of us. We took our number and sat outside. The atmosphere gets one of the stars, and the grilled cheese got the other one. Both pizzas were completely black on the bottom. Ridiculous! I didn't have time to get it fixed, because of my silliness in coming 15 minutes early, so we ate the toppings and left. Service was lacking, food was mediocre at best and definitely would not recommend!

text:
Great all around!!! The food was phenomenal and the service was second to none.  When we first showed up I instantly thought there was no way we were going to get seated right away and would have at least a hour wait because the parking lot was packed.  

This place isn't cheap so if you are trying to go out for a quick bite to eat with the family this is not your place.  If you are going out for a special day with your family this is a great place to go.  They have a decent sized kids menu and the kids food is great.  We went for a later dinner and I think the waitress could tell our kids were ready for their food.  As a result of that the kids food came out in less that 10 minutes and was delicious.  

To top it all off this place has the best military discount I have ever seen.  If you are in the military and show your ID card you get 15% off.

text:
Sher e Punjab isn't just a restaurant, it's a way of life.  Look, I've been to all the Indian restaurants in Tucson, and none of them come close to Sher e Punjab.

The food is outstanding and the family that runs it is a class act.  The enforcer (a gentlemanly old Sikh man who is 55-60) keeps things running smoothly.  His sons will make you feel welcome, even if you are a sociopath or otherwise rejected by society.

While it is true that the shopping center is a haven for those down on their luck, I think they are all panhandling for the $7 they need to eat like kings and queens at Sher e Punjab.

text: Came here for the first time last week -- excellent food for a brewery; my vegetarian friend was completely satisfied with the menu options. Great beer too -- I've had the Blonde many times I've visited Arizona -- my only (small) gripe is the lack of beer descriptions anywhere (no printed list, and the chalkboard tap list doesn't elaborate). The bar was slammed, and it would've been nice to try something new without having to slow service down for everyone by asking what exactly is a "NCAAle" when it could've just been written down somewhere. That said, great place overall, and I'd definitely return.

text:
Claim Jumper fills a definite, profitable niche. While serving well-made delicious food is not the function here, it still manages to provide the trappings of a fine restaurant for those who would not have a good time were they provided authentic food. 

As mentioned in several reviews, the service is excellent. The dark cavernous interior transports patrons to a ski lodge of yesteryear. The furniture is oversized and comfortable. You get the feeling you're somewhere special when you walk in.

The menu is American, designed to be non-threatening and fun. The target demographic has breakfast at McDonald's, lunch at Applebee's, girls night out at the TGIFridays, and now, for a little splurge, must choose between Olive Garden, Red Lobster, or this place. 

Dinner was  $83 including tip -- that's for 3 entrees (1 BBQ ribs, 1 Black Tie pasta, Hill Country salad), 2 beers, and 2 iced teas. Hefty.

For some entertaining reading on sodium levels, check out the nutrition stats on their website: http://www.claimjumper.com/menu_nutritional_information.aspx

text:
Did my research before my last visit to Tucson and I saw this place had lots of reviews for having opened so recently. Prep & Pastry is kind of how I would want my home to be--warm, modern but not pretentious and totally about the pastries. I sat at the counter. So, I got to enjoy how they have a big window into the kitchen and you can see the pastry chef hard at work. Unfortunately, it's too noisy and too far to talk to her, not that I would want to disrupt her baking alchemy. I love the wooden floating shelves by the window where the have everything from plants to an egg beater.

I ordered the duck confit hash, which was solid. I think for my own preference, it could have used more salt but the duck part was very tender. The other ingredients seemed very fresh. One of the owners, Nate, started chatting with me. He was super-friendly and I think because I was by myself and watched the pastry chef bring out a tray of fresh cronut-knockoffs, he offered me one gratis. So I had the maple-glazed with crumbled, real bacon which was very yummy. I tried to ration it off but I finished the other half that same day despite swearing I'd at least wait til the next morning. The pastry chef is also a really friendly gal. She said she was looking for ideas for other cronut flavors and I just randomly made suggestions. 

This place used to be some kind of tea room so they've kept up the tradition by offering afternoon tea. I don't live in Tucson so next time I come, I will for sure make time to go there and perhaps try the tea.
sentences: Did my research before my last visit to Tucson and I saw this place had lots of reviews for having opened so recently.|Prep & Pastry is kind of how I would want my home to be--warm, modern but not pretentious and totally about the pastries.|I sat at the counter.|So, I got to enjoy how they have a big window into the kitchen and you can see the pastry chef hard at work.|Unfortunately, it's too noisy and too far to talk to her, not that I would want to disrupt her baking alchemy.|I love the wooden floating shelves by the window where the have everything from plants to an egg beater.|I ordered the duck confit hash, which was solid.|I think for my own preference, it could have used more salt but the duck part was very tender.|The other ingredients seemed very fresh.|One of the owners, Nate, started chatting with me.|He was super-friendly and I think because I was by myself and watched the pastry chef bring out a tray of fresh cronut-knockoffs, he offered me one gratis.|So I had the maple-glazed with crumbled, real bacon which was very yummy.|I tried to ration it off but I finished the other half that same day despite swearing I'd at least wait til the next morning.|The pastry chef is also a really friendly gal.|She said she was looking for ideas for other cronut flavors and I just randomly made suggestions.|This place used to be some kind of tea room so they've kept up the tradition by offering afternoon tea.|I don't live in Tucson so next time I come, I will for sure make time to go there and perhaps try the tea.

text:
BFF and I had been dying to try this place, and this was our first visit.
Got there at 9:00, just before the rush and were seated on the patio right away by the friendly hostesses (hostii?). Promptly greeted with water and menus. For drinks, a fuzzy navel mimosa and a meinkes mayor - both delicious and a good value at $5 and $6, respectively. For breakfast I ordered the monte cristo, while my friend ordered the sweet potato hash. We were unimpressed with each menu item; both were bland and it seemed everything was undercooked from the potatoes in the country hash to the brioche in the monte cristo.
Consensus: I'll give it another chance, as some other entrees passing by our table looked enviable, and the service is super duper. Look out, A.M. Relleno, you're on my list!

text:
Hands down, one of my favorite place to have brunch around town! 

My last visit I had "The chef omelet with a side of country hash" - which changes daily.
The mint leaves was a great touch to my delicious omelet.  

My favorite dish would be the "Biscuits and Gravy". Cheddar biscuit, sausage, smothered with duck fat gravy and two over easy egg on top. Need I say more?

Of course, you can't come to P&P and not have one of their famous Bloody Mary's! The Bloody Mary is loaded with ingredients: tomato juice, Worcestershire sauce, Tabasco sauce, fresh lemon or lime juice, horseradish, black pepper, and celery salt -- each contributing to "a blend of hundreds of flavor compounds that act on the taste senses". Well P&P sure knows how to hit all taste senses with their version of "The Bloody Mary"

The wait staff here is on point, always so friendly and always make sure our glasses are never empty. 

Looking forward to the next visit!

KCYE
Don V.

text:
My hubby and I ate here for lunch since we had a gift card to use.  We read all the reviews and still decided to try it.  We arrived at 11 am when they opened and were promptly seated.  

Our waitress Penelope came to our table pretty promptly too, to take our drink orders.  She was very patient with me while I was trying to decide what I wanted to order.

While waiting for our soups, Penelope brought their cheese bread, which was quite yummy!  I ordered the cocoMojito (I think that's the name) which was quite yummy too.  A bit too much ice for the price we paid but still delicious.

Hubby and I shared the creamy chicken tortilla soup and the New England clam chowder.  Both were very good.  

Penelope gave us another serving of bread because we devoured the first (I was hungry, lol) and our water was always refilled.  She even apologized for accidentally putting too much ice in hubby's glass.  That was sweet, though he didn't mind at all.  

Our entrees arrived and the potions were a good amount.  Since I did not know what I wanted, I ordered the Widow Maker, because that was one of the sandwiches Hubby was debating.  That thing was HUGE!!!  I took out the two onion rings and gave them to my hubby.  The negative I will say about the burger is that we ordered it to be cooked well done.  Part of it still had pink, which was the part we did not eat.  

Hubby ordered the tri tip and that was quite yummy!  Penelope knew we were sharing our entrees, so she brought an extra bowl of the au jous.  She didn't have to do it but she did.  And we greatly appreciated that.

Hubby told her it was my birthday and she brought out a red velvet cupcake with a candle on it for me!  She even brought two forks for me to share....so sweet!  

The manager on duty checked on us and chatted for a bit.

The decor was nice and the place was clean.

We spent less than $50 for an alcoholic drink, two bowls of soup and two entrees.  A bit pricey, maybe.  But the food was good.  I think, though, we would come back on special occasions.

text:
Overall experience at penca was good. The foot itself was tasty and the drink selection was nice. 
Margarita was less sweet than I'd expect and the selection of sangrias were nice. The carnitas taco I found more like a seca meat and was super dry and got stuck in between my teeth. The carne Asada is good also but the stand out was the mole. Super moist and tender meat. Salsa and guac board was decent but I thought the pico lacked flavor. Finally the churros. The churros themselves I found too doughy in the centers. They were nice and crispy on the outside but the centers were pools of uncooked dough. 

Service wise it was hit and miss. I ordered some chicharones pork rinds but they never came. Also the wait was so long in between the salsas and the entrees the waiter brought more guac because he felt bad. No excuses for them here, the place was basically empty. 

The food hit or miss included with the service mishaps gave this place a 3 star. Might be a more consistent experience down the street at poca cosa or el charro.

text:
This is such a cute place and the owners are so friendly.

Went in with my Groupon for the patio wine tasting.  There were decent pours and cheese and pretzels to snack on. 

I really enjoyed the tasting overall, however, it isn't very cool in the tasting room.  So it can get a little uncomfortable.  

I dig that the majority of bottles are under $15 here and I love supporting the local business.

I recommend anyone to go check it out on a free afternoon.  Just beware, parking can be difficult if they are busy.

text:
The service was great and very friendly. The atmosphere INSIDE is perfect for taking someone on a date or catching up with a friend. Very intimate setting. Parking was great and got a spot right when we got there, after 5pm on weekdays its free. Thats a huge plus especially for a restaurant in downtown.

The butter for the bread was yum, it just spreads on so easily and makes it hard for you not to stop asking for more bread to use it on. (The amazing butter is not sold in the market so enjoy it)

For appetizer we had Barrio Bruschetta, it had locally grown figs, yummy prosciutto, and perfect amount of argula. It was delicious, will order again.

For the entrees, we had the Lamb Shank and Bouillabaisse. The lamb shank just fell off the bone and was the best lamb I've had. Loved the touch of charred brussel sprouts. The Bouillabaisse, the broth was wow. I would have as much of the broth in the beginning, since the broth didnt continously have that yummy factor as it continued to stew since its very warm. But besides that, the seafood was delicious. Will order both entrees again, very suggested. Can't wait to have the left overs with rice at home.

For dessert, we shared the creme brûlée. 

Ps. Dont forget to draw on the table with the provided crayons.

text: There are not too many good Indian restaurants in Tucson, and from the outside I wasn't expecting much since it's in a little strip mall next to a 99Cent store.  I went for dinner on 09-11-14.  Once inside i was pleasantly surprised as it was very clean and a nice atmosphere.  I ordered Chicken Vindaloo with Basmati Rice and Naan.  The Rice was cooked perfectly and the Naan was also great, with a nice charred texture from the Tandoori oven. The chicken Vindaloo was prepared nicely with fresh cilantro and a flavorful sauce.  The Vindaloo was made with dark meat which i do like, but i would prefer some white meat from the chicken as well.  The prices were reasonable for dinner, but also is a la carte, so the rice and Naan were not included in the meal price.  Either way the prices are very good here and nice size portions.

Best Indian food in Tucson, bar none. Great for vegetarians and meat lovers too. I'd say try anything with lamb in it because there aren't many places around here that even serve lamb. And of course you should always wash your meal down with their amazing mango lassi, such a guilty pleasure. 
It doesn't help that I'm writing this review on an empty stomach, but trust me when I say that Punjab will go above and beyond the call of duty to fill your gut with goodness. I think the overall thing that I like best about this place is the sheer feeling of comfort that comes from everything. Modest atmosphere and comfort food served with gingerly kindness. It's like what momma used to make, if she had originally immigrated from India. The pricing is fair, not too expensive, and well worth the portions you'd be receiving. 
So yeah, go take a friend here and enjoy the food, or help yourself to some takeout. In the end, you'll be plenty satisfied.

text:
My family and I came here for dinner last Saturday.  I reserved a table online which was so easy and convenient.  If you're coming here for dinner on the weekend, I would highly recommend making a reservation.  The restaurant is gorgeous, it's a renovated train station with the most beautiful patio right by the tracks.  We were able to sit at a booth by the window, which was very nice.  There is also a stunning bar which has every drink imaginable.  My only criticism of the venue is that the lighting is way too dim at night.  I love the dark, sultry vibe, but it was a little hard to read my menu.

Maynards is upscale and quite expensive, so dress appropriately and be prepared to pay more.  It's the perfect place to celebrate a special occasion with a small group.

Our waitress (young lady with short, blonde hair) couldn't have been better, she was so sweet and attentive.  She made excellent food and beverage selections and comped my glass of wine when it was spilled, no questions asked.

To eat, we started with the baked mushroom (mushrooms and goat cheese in a pastry shell) and the heirloom tomato salad.  Both were very delicious, but the tomato salad could have used a bit more cheese.  For dinner, my brother had the pork schnitzel, my mom had the wild salmon and I had the mushroom risotto.  Both my mom and brother agreed that their dishes were fantastic.  However, I was rather disappointed with the texture and presentation of my risotto.  The flavor was nice, but it was pretty watery and was served on a plate, rather than in a bowl.  I've never had risotto served on a plate before, it seemed quite odd.  To drink, I had a glass of white sangria and the Chartron la Fleur Bordeaux, both were excellent.  

Overall, I had a wonderful experience at Maynards and I would definitely come again.  I'm pleased that they have added more vegetarian dishes to the menu, perhaps they could just tweak the risotto and tomato salad.

categories: Beer, Wine & Spirits, Bars, Pubs, Nightlife, Beer Gardens, Food, Piano Bars
attributes.Ambience: {'touristy': False, 'hipster': False, 'romantic': False, 'divey': True, 'intimate': False, 'trendy': False, 'upscale': False, 'classy': True, 'casual': False}
text:
The Dusty Monk is an European style pub downtown Tucson. It is located in the same courtyard as La Cocina. You can also enter it directly from the street. 

They have an amazing selection of beer. I first visited during the World Cup, before they were officially open, their soft opening. I remember I had a Franziskaner Hefe-Weisse. They had it on draft, it is one of my all-time favorite beers. They have now rotated their taps.

This last visit I had two beers, both draft. I started with a Palm Ale and then had a lighter Estrella Damm. Both of the beers were served in their specialty glasses. These were both new beers for me and I loved them. There is a sign outside the front door that advertises beer from $2.00 - $24.00. I love the range. 

The Dusty Monk also specializes in Whisky. They have a multi-page menu and offer delicious sounding Whisky cocktails. 

We really enjoyed Don the bartender, and I believe also the owner. He is a great conversationalist and good company.

This pub is open Tuesday through Saturday. They open at 4pm. I will be back soon and I will bring friends. The Dusty Monk is one of my favorite places in Tucson. I really enjoy it.

text:
What I like about this place: 
- Great overall decor and ambience. The booths and all the wood have a very warm and welcoming feel. 
- The location is great. A nice alternative to Congress or District (my usual haunts) that still feels adult and trendy.
- The service is great. I have mostly always sat at the bar and been served by Cassidy (a staunch New Orleans Saints fan). She's so friendly with everyone but still manages to stay on top of delivering speedy service. 
- I love the focus on local everything.... 

Why the 3 stars then?

I wish the overall selection for wine and food was greater. The beer selection seems great (but I'm not much of a beer drinker so I would not be the best judge) but the wine selection is pretty limited. I totally get the emphasis on local wines and wine by the tap, its just that if you don't care for the two or three wines they serve, you're sort of out of luck. For their food offerings, nothing stands out as particularly good or bad and the menu is a bit limited. So I'm not downgrading them for food, but neither can I call it a plus.

text: Brunch on a Sunday entailed a 1.5 hour wait for a table since they don't take reservations (we went to the Botanical Gardens about 10 min away to kill some time), then an additional 30 minute wait for the food to arrive after we ordered. This seems to be a common theme in Tucson for our dining experiences, which is unfortunate. Grilled cheese was awesome - hits the spot if you're looking for some smokey-gooey-goodness. The chicken and french toast were ok. Pretty much an entire chicken breast is presented, along with rosemary brioche french toast. The french toast was super doughy - yummy, but really filling. I wished they had let the bread soak in more of the egg. The fried chicken was not to my liking, as I didn't prefer the outer crust flavors (and would have preferred chicken thighs and dark meat rather than chicken breast to give it a more complex flavor.)

text:
Came here again and had some delicious food again.  It appears that this place is French inspired and offers up some good eats to the people of Tucson.  

We started with the baked mushroom which is essentially mushroom stuffed into puff pastry and covered with a delicious sauce.  For our main meals, I had the New York strip and my wife chose the house made pasta.  Both were delicious and we ate every morsel on our plates.  They do offer a tasting menu if  you call 1-2 days ahead of time giving the chef to personalize your meal.  This sounds like a fun thing to do and maybe we will do it next time we are in town. 

This would make a nice place for a special dinner and located right next to the train tracks.  You would think that you are getting ready to board the next train after a nice meal.

text:
This chain restaurant keeps up its reputation of offering delicious, fatty food in huge portions in Tucson. 

From appetizer to dessert, everything on the menu can be shared because the portions are SO big. Definitely come with a friend and plan on splitting everything unless you have a large waistline. 

Service is prompt and friendly, and plates are priced accordingly with their size. If you're in the mood for some down-home cooking in a pleasant environment - and you feel you could eat a buffalo - come to Claim Jumper.

It's just too bad the northwest side needs something like this only better.

text:
I had the beet salad with arugula and wild mushrooms which was excellent in every respect, followed by the pork chop. This turned out to be two frenched double thickness pork chops artfully arranged with asparagus and a slice of polenta- it was perhaps the best pork chop I've ever had. 

A word of disappointment: One of my party ordered the 6oz steak medium rare, and it was delivered medium, although this was corrected. 

Also, the Maynard's Manhattan is pretty darn amazing. 

Lastly, they serve Poutine. In Tucson. I didn't see that coming.

text:
I made a terrible decision to come here one day.  Terrible because now all I can think of is when will I be eating their tacos again.  I want Street everyday.

I was reluctant when I walked in my first time and see that it was like a Chipotle in that you build your own taco down the line.  But as a picky eater, I was ecstatic because it meant ordering would be simpler.

Once I took a bite of their carne asada tacos, I knew I finally found MY taco place.  Since moving to Tucson 3 years ago, I've been having a surprising amount of difficulty finding a taco place that reminded me of what I ate in Chicago.  I finally found it.

I would give them a 4 stars because it's downtown and Jesus H Christ parking is a pain in the ass.  They need a more central location.  But I love them so much, I'm not going to bring their rating down.

PLEASE open a location out of the downtown area.  Or get some parking!

text: I came here for the wings on my self proclaimed wing tour of Tucson. I had only ever been to Trident as a college student and when campus was no longer my hub, I hadn't returned in lets just say a while.  I remember the food being cheap but good so I was excited to bring my husband who had never been.  We ordered two types of wings, I don't even remember, because sadly they weren't memorable and a burger.  The wings were undercooked and had no flavor just salty.  Don't get me wrong, I am a love of all things salty, but these were too much even for me.  The burgers we so so and not worth coming back.  I was sad, because it spoiled the college "memories" I had of this place. I will try again in a few months and hope it was just a really off day in the kitchen. The environment was nice, it was busy for a random weeknight and it was damn cold inside, so its a great place to escape the heat and grab a beer and some food other than burgers and wings, in my humble opinion.

text:
Brunch... Most of the time you can't mess it up so most places get exceptional reviews for bacon, eggs and mimosas. 

Not to hype it up but this place transcends brunch. We sat at the bar and chatted with the keep for recommendations and had such a hard time choosing because everything looked a-MAZ-ing. We ended up with pork belly Benedict and a monte cristo sipping on what I would call the best Bloody Mary I ever had. Yes they use a zing zang base but have a specially made secret mix added to it. I wish I had more room in my stomach for the freshly made cronuts (yes, half croissant half donut) but thank cod for next weekend :) 

Really cool atmosphere, if bloody Mary's aren't your thing they have twists on classic breakfast cocktails... This place is a must try!

text: Fun, fun, fun.  So popular, you may have to wait--but who wants to eat at a restaurant that has no patrons??  Plus, there's plenty to do in the little shopping center: a phenomenal antique store, Zoe, a great dress shop and a native seed store.  Love the avocado and eggs dish--very innovative and topped with interesting herbs.  Very lively with a hip atmosphere missing in so many Tucson dining spots.

text:
All I can say is what happened? We used to go to this place when it was located on Harrison and 22nd. They moved and we were really bummed. We have been dining here since they moved and it seems to have gone down hill. We thought it was just growing pains so we have over looked some things but last night was it for us. 

We ordered two appetizers ( Sw Potato Fries and Fried Zucchini) 
Grilled Chicken platter with side salad
Steak platter for husband
Hot tea and Iced tea

Appetizers never came out
Salads were served but were the very basic for 2.99 Iceberg Lettuce, a sliver or two of onion, tomato slice and a small little thing of ranch dressing. 
barely through my small dinner salad the plates with dinner came out and were delivered by our waitress as if they were coming in for a landing as she put them on the table without waiting for anyone to move a single plate that they were eating from. 
ice tea was not refilled, my hot tea was not refilled nor was i asked if I would like more hot water. 

I didn't eat much of my dinner but out waitress would not have noticed.We saw her plenty when we were between the salad and dinner but  we did not see her after plates were served. We waited and waited for a ticket , she looked our direction and could not tell we were ready to leave i guess so I flagged another waitress who noticed the uneaten meal and asked if I wanted a box. I told her no, the chicken was dry, veggies were drowned in butter and mushy. Just a check please. I also pointed out the appetizers were not delivered. Our waitress did come by with the check at last and offered free desert and did remove charges for my meal and appetizers which was fabulous. I would say I would give it another go but previous experiences combined with other reviews and our experience last night make this a NO GO for future outings. Off we go to find another favorite.  

Kickers was it wasn't that busy!!! hmmm....

text:
I'm surprised by the high reviews for this place. Tucson must really be hurting for good breweries if this place is popular. None of their beers are particularly noteworthy, though the Hefeweizen is pretty good. The raspberry and Tucson Blonde were awful.

Barrio brews the beer served at Gentle Ben's, which is next to the UA campus. One very odd thing we noticed is that a couple of the beers at Gentle Ben's tasted better than the ones served at Barrio. That kind of inconsistency is not a good sign.

We didn't eat at Barrio, so I can't speak for their food. But if you're looking for a good craft beer, I wouldn't bother with this place.

This is my third visit to Maynards. This is my first review of it.  My first visit was good. My second visit was fair. This last time was terrible.

We arrived on time for our reservation but had to wait 20 minutes to be seated.  Service was very slow and lousy.  Martini was a half filled glass.  Bread came after our salads.   The Porkchop  was fatty and small.  My daughter had the duck -  it was bloody rare and had to be sent back.  Management did not even follow up on the problem.  

Overall a terrible experience.  I am done with this place.

text:
I've eaten lunch here in the past and it has been very good.  Last night (a quiet Wednesday) was another story.  Four of us went after work for a birthday get-together.  We ordered simple items -- not the entrees.  It took 45 minutes to get the quesadillas which consisted of 4 small flour tortilla folded in half with maybe a teaspoon of cheese inside -- totally underwhelming.  My friend had a salad with seemed to consist of simply bagged lettuce with 2 slices of avocado and then we had to ask for dressing since it looked undressed.  The elote was ridiculous -- two dried ears of corn on a skewer which had been charred with really no sauce, cheese or anything.  There were maybe 8 other people in the restaurant and there were plenty of servers, so I don't quite understand why service was so slow -- other than the owner may have not been present.  

The restaurant is really nice and the ambience/atmosphere is fun -- but I wouldn't go back for that alone -- I need some tasty food and better service.

P.S.  Happy hour ends at 5 pm -- most people work till 5:00 downtown.

categories: Mexican, Restaurants, Nightlife, Bars, Venues & Event Spaces, Event Planning & Services

text:
I was pretty excited about this place as I liked the description of their food, however, I can't say the food actually impressed or satisfied me. I order a plate of tacos along with some chicharron appetizer.  First, the chicharron, was just okay. It was really basic chicharron with a little bit of spice. At that point, I was hoping the tacos had a little more zing. Unfortunately, the order of 2 tacos for around 10 dollars was not as good as I had hoped. The tacos themselves were excellent. They had a lot of flavor and the ingredients were fresh but they were small. The rice and bean portions were also really small. Overall, the meal felt unsatisfactory as nothing really did it for me on a variety of different levels.

If you go to any other good mexican restaurant around town like, Pico de Gallo, which is amazing and cheaper then penca, or Botana, which is equally delicious as Pico, you'll find much better meals for cheaper. Also, at these other establishments you can pick how many and  which kind of tacos you want. I felt like Penca limiting you to one type of meat is unwarranted. There was no option in the menu to get more.  

I also ordered orchata, which was really good but the portion size was, again, also really small. I think the problem with Penca right now is that they are limiting themselves too much with portions and variety. I do get the sense that they want to provide better ingredients and good atmosphere, however I think it detracts too much from the overall satisfaction of the food. To be fair, I think this place can go from okay to amazing if they did some adjustments in portion size, menu flexibility and flavor adjustments.

categories: Pubs, Bars, Wine Bars, Nightlife, Cocktail Bars

text: Been here a couple times now, and both experiences have been pretty good. This place sits right in between Diablo Burger and The Rialto, but can be easy to miss as it's well hidden along the building facade ~ think "speakeasy". The interior is dimly lit with dark accents, and has ample room at the bar, as well as booth table seating. The menu consists of a number of draft beers and local wines, as well as a few odd concoctions of highly stylized sangrias as well as "beer presses" (with coffee, and tea). They also have a limited menu of food options, with the Fried Pickles being my favorite ~ seriously, get them, they're incredible. On my most recent visit, my friend tried the "Melo Yelo" white sangria, which we both agreed tasted a little sour / pickled. We notified the bartender, who agreed as well and was eager to make it up to us by replacing with one of their wines by-the-glass. The service, while very friendly and accommodating, can tend to be slow (especially at peak hours).

name: Prep & Pastry
categories: Restaurants, Cocktail Bars, Bars, Comfort Food, American (New), Sandwiches, Nightlife, Breakfast & Brunch

text:
Perhaps I need to give Prep and Pastry a second shot, because my first visit was not as amazing as the reviews would suggest.  I must give them a lot of credit for their unique menu, impressive bakery and extensive array of brunch cocktails.  I also loved the rustic venue, with its high ceilings and wood floors.  Our waiter was a delight, he really made our experience a pleasant one.  

My only criticism is the food.  I was told that I have to order the french toast because it's their specialty.  I ending up getting the french toast of the day, which had whipped cream, blueberries, sweet cream cheese and brioche bread.  To begin with, the portion was way too big.  They gave me four huge slices and I'm the type of person who prefers less food, so that I can enjoy my meal without feeling engorged.  The texture of the french toast was too dry, it seems like they didn't soak it in the custard long enough.  There were very few blueberries and the combo of whipped cream and cream cheese was just too rich.  Maybe I ordered the wrong item!

My mom had the a.m. relleno which was the perfect portion size and very tasty, but a little too spicy.  My brother ordered the monte cristo with a side of biscuits and gravy.  Again, the portion was out of control!  Two giant sandwiches that were impossible to eat, with a ton of potatoes.  Altogether, just too heavy.  As for the drinks, my coffee and peach/orange mimosa was delicious, but the red sangria was far too sweet.

To top it off, this place is crazy busy on the weekend.  My family and I got there at 9am, so we only had to wait about 20 minutes.  After 10am, it was a zoo.

I'm probably in the minority here, but I think Prep and Pastry is trying too hard to impress their customers with lots of flavors and huge portions.  They should scale back and simplify.  I know it's brunch and it's supposed to be decadent, but you have to strike a balance.  I think I'll stick with B-line for breakfast.

text:
After hearing about this place I was excited to try it and looking forward to another great experience downtown. The first time I went it was just after 9, and there was only a limited late night menu available. I understand the late night idea, but there was no mention of this on the website, and since I was looking forward to really exploring the menu I decided to wait. 

The second time I returned was around 3pm on a Sunday, which they close at 3. Odd hours, but they are listed on their site, chalk it up to my not paying attention.

The third time was around 8 pm on a weeknight, and we were seated right next to the kitchen although there were several open tables. I went with a buddy of mine, and we weren't wearing nice jeans and button downs, I felt like if we had we would have received a better table. After ordering two apps and two entrees, the waitress asked why I didn't want to be more adventurous (I ordered a carne asada taco and a carnitas taco). I was kind of offended, if I wanted to be more "adventurous" I would have been, with or without your suggestion, and certainly not after the question was phrased very pretentiously. If you can make great carne asada and carnitas tacos, I would be totally ok with returning and trying the lengua. 
Chips and salse were brought prior to the appetizers, there were two salsas and they were both good. Keep in mind we were two hungry guys, and when we asked for more chips the response verbally was yes, but body language and tone was "oh great, these guys just want to fill up on chips and salsa." The appetizers arrived and the Queso Fundido was great, the tortillas served on the side were also very good. The Guacamole was bland, and a really a small portion, that was also served with the same salsas that came complimentary, do yourself a favor and just skip this, I feel like I paid too much for half of a mashed up avocado, and again the salsas were the stars.
When the entrees arrived the tacos were great, the sides were really disappointing. They had almost no flavor, and after salting them they just tasted like salt. So you're paying $6/taco, and they were good, but there are plenty of taco stands that offer the same quality. 
So we were kinda disappointed, and I guess you could tell because when the manager walked by and asked how everything was (knowing well that as a new business, the feedback is vital to moving forward and correcting any mistakes) I mentioned that the food was bland, but also what I did like. The response was "OK, I guess I need to try that then." To sum it all up, service was mediocre at best, and the food was good at points and then also pretty bad at points. I am looking forward to trying this again, I hope things have improved since, and if they have a new review will reflect that.
